summary: An Azure Storage Account is a container that holds all Azure Storage data objects including blobs, files, queues, and tables, accessible globally over HTTP/HTTPS. It provides a unique namespace and offers multiple account types optimized for different workloads and performance needs. Data is durable, highly available, secure, and massively scalable.

key_concepts.explanation: The default and recommended storage account type supporting blobs, files, queues, and tables with the widest redundancy options including LRS, GRS, ZRS, and GZRS.
SSD-backed account type optimized for high transaction rates, small objects, or consistently low latency; supports block and append blobs only.
SSD-backed account type for Azure Files only, supporting both SMB and NFS protocols; suited for enterprise or high-performance file workloads.
SSD-backed account type exclusively for page blobs, typically used for VM disks and scenarios requiring random read/write access.
Names must be 3-24 characters, lowercase letters and numbers only, and globally unique across all of Azure.

LRS (single region, 3 copies), GRS (two regions), ZRS (three availability zones), GZRS (zones + secondary region), RA-GRS/RA-GZRS (read access to secondary). ZRS and GZRS are not available for all account types.
You cannot change a storage account type after creation; you must create a new account and copy data to it.

exam_focus_points: Know which storage services (blob, file, queue, table) are supported by each account type
Standard General-Purpose v2 is the recommended default for most scenarios
Premium account types use SSDs and are designed for low latency and high throughput
ZRS and GZRS are only available for specific account types and regions — not all account types support zone redundancy
Storage account names must be globally unique, 3-24 chars, lowercase alphanumeric only
You cannot convert a storage account from one type to another — you must create new and migrate data
Premium File Shares is the only account type supporting both SMB and NFS file shares
Data Lake Storage is built on top of Blob Storage (not a separate storage service type)
similar_concepts: Azure Managed Disks (often confused with Premium Page Blobs for VM storage)
Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (built on Blob Storage, not a separate account type)
Azure Files vs Azure Blob Storage (both store data but for different access patterns)
LRS vs ZRS vs GRS vs GZRS redundancy options
common_mistakes: Thinking you can change a storage account type after creation — you cannot, you must create a new one and copy data
Assuming all redundancy options (ZRS, GZRS) are available for all account types — they are not available for all types or regions
Confusing Premium Block Blobs (for blobs) with Premium File Shares (for files) — they support different storage services
Thinking Data Lake Storage is a separate storage account type — it is a capability set built on top of Blob Storage
Using uppercase letters or special characters in storage account names — only lowercase alphanumeric is allowed
Assuming Premium storage accounts support all storage services — each Premium type supports only a subset
